danger_signals.visual_form: varied colorful bullet patterns typical of shmup genre
organized enemy sprite groupings moving in coordinated patterns
danger_signals.meaning: escalating difficulty and concentrated attack zones

danger_signals.visual_form: large sprite characters with distinctive visual presentation distinct from standard enemies
danger_signals.meaning: major threat requiring pattern recognition and sustained engagement

safety_signals.visual_form: collectible objects dropped by enemies or existing in environment
safety_signals.meaning: temporary or permanent ability enhancement reducing relative danger

safety_signals.visual_form: empty playfield areas without active projectiles
safety_signals.meaning: safe zones for repositioning and recovery between attack waves

safety_signals.visual_form: colorful, cartoonish aesthetic with absurdist enemy designs
safety_signals.meaning: comedic framing reduces psychological threat perception compared to serious shmups

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: centered screen position, player-controlled movement patterns

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: diverse parody-based enemy designs referencing Gradius series and other Konami franchises
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: varied enemy types with distinct visual silhouettes enabling quick threat classification
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: spawn from edges and upper screen area; movement patterns indicate attack intent

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: stable playfield with regular enemy waves, consistent background scrolling
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemies spawn in predictable patterns; attack intensity maintains steady escalation

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: large sprite boss centered on screen; background may shift or freeze
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: boss exhibits complex attack patterns; other enemy spawning typically pauses or reduces

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: player weapon visual changes (altered projectile appearance or spread pattern)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: increased firepower output; player offense capability visibly amplified through feedback

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: potential loss of active power-ups shown through weapon visual reversion
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: gameplay capability regression; player must recollect power-ups to restore effectiveness

environmental_storytelling.example: enemy designs and backgrounds reference Gradius and other Konami properties, communicating the game's self-aware 'cute 'em up' positioning within series canon
colorful, non-threatening visual presentation of dangerous gameplay elements reduces tension and signals comedic intent over serious challenge
multiple selectable protagonists (likely including character parodies) signal player agency and variety in how the player approaches the world

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: follows scripted movement patterns; fires projectiles in player direction or geometric patterns
displays complex multi-phase attack patterns; occupies significant screen real estate; exhibits dramatic behavior shifts indicating phase transitions
weapon visual and audio feedback intensifies; projectile spread or frequency increases noticeably
visual regression to base weapon configuration; movement mechanics unchanged but offensive capability diminished

notes: Parodius: Non-Sense Fantasy is a 1995 Super Famicom release in the Parodius cute 'em up parody series. The game communicates world-state primarily through genre-standard shmup mechanics: projectile density indicates difficulty escalation, power-up acquisition visibly modifies player offense capability, and boss encounters represent distinct world-state transitions. The game's visual communication relies heavily on colorful, cartoonish aesthetics and parody-based enemy designs that reference Gradius and other Konami franchises. Political or faction systems are not present; danger/safety communication is purely mechanical. The absurdist visual tone serves as a meta-communicative safety signal, framing the experience as comedic rather than threatening despite mechanical challenge. Limited empirical data available; assessment based on genre conventions and series positioning.
